Daniel Propp

May 11th, 2026 ( Foreign Policy - Delivered by Newstex )

Federal climate policy has effectively ceased to function in the United States. Since retaking power last year, the Trump administration has relentlessly and systematically stripped back incentives and regulations aimed at reducing greenhouse gas emissions.

On the foreign-policy front, the United States is now absent not only from the Paris Agreement, but also the International Panel on Climate Change, the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) (officially as of January 2027), and most other international fora concerned with the climate crisis. The administration has also dismantled many federal offices that implement climate policy, including the State Department's Office of the Special Presidential Envoy for Climate, the Department of Energy's Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy, and the White House's Climate Policy Office.

It is trite but still worthwhile to note that the impacts of these actions will extend far beyond the next U.S. election. As greenhouse gases continue to accumulate in the atmosphere, the effects of climate change-including extreme weather, sea level rise, and disease outbreaks-will make Americans poorer, sicker, and more vulnerable.

The next president will face the daunting challenge of rebuilding U.S. climate policy. But amid the wreckage left by the Trump administration, the next leader will also have more latitude than any in recent memory to reimagine how the U.S. government addresses the climate crisis. The next U.S. administration would do well not only to learn from previous policy regimes, but also to reconsider the very concept of 'climate policy.'

Protesters in Cleveland, Ohio, in 1970. Bettman Archive/via Getty Images

On the heels of successive victories in the 1970s, the U.S. environmental movement-heretofore concerned primarily with local pollution-found itself confronting a challenge of global proportions. Advances in climate science, most famously highlighted by Dr. James Hansen's 1988 testimony to Congress, showed that greenhouse gases released by fossil fuel combustion were changing the composition of the atmosphere, with potentially catastrophic and worldwide consequences.

Alarm over global warming, later termed climate change, motivated a raft of policies to slow greenhouse gas emissions. The U.S. government began subsidizing wind and solar energy in the hope that they would eventually supplant more carbon-intensive sources of electricity. It also signed onto the UNFCCC, laying the groundwork for decades of international coordination on climate.

As scientists' understanding of the climate grew, so did the realm of what was considered climate policy. Carbon dioxide, it turned out, was leaking from nearly every sector of the economy, including cars, power plants, factories, farms, and landfills. And carbon dioxide wasn't the only cause of climate change; so-called super pollutants such as methane and hydrofluorocarbons were also raising temperatures. It became clear that mitigating climate change would require addressing a multitude of emissions sources. It would have to be an all-of-society effort.

Efforts to address emissions, too, spawned their own complexities. Carbon-free energy would require more copper and lithium, demanding increased attention to these supply chains. The uneven burden of-and responsibility for-climate change demanded that policy interventions consider preexisting inequities. All the while, new tensions emerged as nations stumbled unevenly towards emissions reductions.

By the time U.S. President Joe Biden took office, the slate of climate-related issues facing policymakers had grown dizzyingly broad. Recognizing this, Biden pledged a 'whole-of-government' approach to climate change, enlisting the capabilities and portfolios of each federal agency. He established a Climate Policy Office within the White House to coordinate among them. Led first by Gina McCarthy and then by Ali Zaidi, this small team became the heartbeat of the Biden administration's climate platform.

Then-U.S. President Joe Biden announces a $7 billion 'Solar For All' program at a park in Triangle, Virginia, on April 22, 2024. Andrew Harnik/Getty Images

The approach yielded major victories. The Biden administration announced an ambitious new emissions reduction target, reoriented federal procurement toward low-carbon technologies, tightened vehicle emissions standards, imposed new restrictions on coal power plants, created a detailed plan for reducing methane emissions, and employed 15,000 young people in the nation's first-ever Climate Corps. Along the way, it obligated more than $97 billion of federal funds to clean energy, electric vehicles, conservation, and other provisions of the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA). If preserved, these interventions would have prevented hundreds of millions of tons of greenhouse gases from entering the atmosphere.

The approach also encountered challenges. The Department of Energy may have spent as little as 39 percent of its IRA funding before the clock ran out on Biden's term. The Loan Programs Office, the workhorse of the department's support for clean energy, ended the administration with more than 160 outstanding loan applications worth more than $200 billion. Among the reasons for the delays, according to Energy Department employees at the time, were frequent and significant interventions by the White House that failed to empower the office to move expeditiously. Others faulted the White House's interest in advancing multiple, sometimes conflicting, aims at once.

Elsewhere, the administration's climate ambitions ran headlong into agency priorities. In 2021, Bidenpledged to end public financing of fossil fuel infrastructure abroad. This announcement caused tension at two public financing agencies, the U.S. Development Finance Corporation and the Export-Import Bank (EXIM). Employees at both organizations viewed their ability to finance natural gas plants as a valuable tool for enhancing electricity access, bolstering diplomatic relations, and displacing dirtier energy sources. Many chafed at what they saw as impractical political restrictions, and EXIM continued to support fossil fuel projects.

Lastly, the procession of top-down, explicitly climate-oriented policies left an easy list of targets for a subsequent administration already intent on unwinding environmental protections. Rather than addressing the systemic drivers of rising electricity prices, the Trump administration blamed them on 'ideologically motivated regulations,' a useful pretext for slashing much of the Biden administration's energy policy.

Under Trump, the White House has reversed the Biden administration's fuel economy standards, repealed its coal power plants rule, nullified its emissions reduction target, stopped enforcing methane regulations, canceled $2.5 billion of the Department of Energy's clean energy awards under the IRA, and attempted to halt wind farm development-all while citing the previous administration for elevating climate concerns over economic opportunity.

Wind turbines generate electricity near Block Island, Rhode Island, on July 7, 2022. John Moore/Getty Images

The delays and transience that characterized the Biden administration's climate agenda were not the results of mismanagement or misplaced priorities. Rather, they were the consequences of an outdated policy paradigm that no longer suits the climate challenge.

Climate policy refers to government actions to address both the negative impacts of human activity on the climate, or mitigation, and the negative impacts of a changing climate on society, or adaptation. In the 1990s, when national governments first came together to address climate change, this definition encompassed a manageable set of interventions. Today, however, the bounds of what constitutes climate policy have grown so broad as to be meaningless.

This is evident in the basic functions of government. Today's architects of climate policy are united by little more than a shared concern about greenhouse gas emissions. The daily concerns of a diplomat negotiating a climate agreement bear a paltry resemblance to those of a program manager allocating sustainable farming grants. The regulator tracking methane emissions from oil and gas wells has little overlap with the administrator setting rules for climate-resilient housing.

All can reasonably be said to be administering climate policy-and indeed their actions materially advance mitigation and adaptation-but there is almost nothing in their quotidian tasks to suggest that they share the same policy domain. Attempting to group these disparate roles under a single umbrella ignores an underlying truth: There is no such thing, functionally or thematically, as climate policy.

Policy domains are defined by a distinct set of concerns, instruments, and implementing agencies. Monetary policy addresses inflation and employment through interest rates, reserve requirements, and discount rates; it flows primarily through the U.S. Federal Reserve. Education policy promotes improved learning outcomes and school stewardship through funding allocation, standards, and research; it is chiefly implemented by the Education Department.

Even foreign policy has a defined issue (events outside the country), a defined set of tools (diplomacy, economic pressure, and military force), and a group of implementing agencies (including but not limited to the State Department, the Defense Department, and the Commerce Department).

That climate change affects nearly every U.S. agency-and nearly every agency has a plausible toolkit for addressing it-suggests that climate policy is no longer a meaningful policy domain.

None of this is to denigrate the urgency of the climate crisis, nor to suggest that responsibility for addressing it should not be shared throughout the U.S. government. Global temperatures are likely to surpass 1.5˚C of warming by the end of the decade, with potentially dire consequences for all facets of life. Seeing climate policy for what it really is-an awkward grouping of distinct policy realms-opens up new opportunities to make federal climate action more potent, more endemic, and more durable.

Protesters at a really near COP21 in Paris on Dec. 12, 2015. Francois Guillot/AFP via Getty Images

The emergence of centrally organized climate action was originally a practical expression of the issue's urgency and scale. After Hansen's congressional testimony, sustained activist campaigns pressed the U.S. government to move decisively, with demands for action intensifying as the window of opportunity grew narrower.

Successive Democratic administrations endeavored to answer the call for bold leadership by proposing and enacting increasingly ambitious and high-visibility policies. Having the White House coordinate and promulgate these policy agendas demonstrated a level of serious intent that could not replicated by quieter and more dispersed interventions, however effective.

Moreover, the nature of the climate crisis itself appeared to lend itself to top-down policymaking. For much of the 21st century, climate progress has found itself crosswise of market forces. The clean energy technologies necessary to limit greenhouse gas emissions could not yet compete on cost with fossil fuels, nor were private investors inclined to splash out the trillions of dollars required to get the economy on track for net zero. It was clear that correcting these market failures would require robust, carefully planned government intervention.

This was not an outcome that individual agencies, or even individual governments, could achieve by acting independently. Climate change was a collective action problem requiring a coordinated solution.

While these pressures remain, the math has shifted. Wind turbines and solar panels are now the most affordable sources of electricity even without government support, and batteries are increasingly cost-competitive with fossil fuels. In some countries, renewables have become sufficiently attractive to trigger a rapid and unplanned energy transition, undercutting the case for centrally planned climate action. Meanwhile, shifting politics and a cost-of-living crisis-exacerbated by high oil prices following the closure of the Strait of Hormuz-have led climate advocates to opt for affordability messaging, reducing pressure on the federal government to foreground climate concerns. Under the current circumstances, grouping climate concerns under a unified policy platformcould undercut rather than advance climate progress.

International climate agreements offer a cautionary example. Nearly every year since 1995, national governments have sent representatives to a summit of the UNFCCC to hammer out a global plan of action. This process has delivered momentous achievements, most famously in 2015, when the landmark Paris Agreement compelled countries to develop increasingly ambitious emissions reductions targets. But the shortcomings of the process were long apparent. Wrangling 195 parties and all greenhouse gas-emitting sectors into a single pledge limited the ambition and enforceability of these agreements, constraining their ability to bend the emissions curve.

Though these agreements provided valuable direction to global climate action, the urgency of the crisis now demands more targeted and concrete interventions. Recent trade disputes with Chinathreatened to choke off U.S. developers' supply of cheap clean energy components, endangering the United States' ability to decarbonize its electricity grid. In this environment, the most important international climate agreement the next administration makes is unlikely to be a UNFCCC accord, but instead a bilateral trade agreement to ensure the continued flow of clean energy technologies.

Absorbing this objective into a more expansive climate agreement would only serve to divert diplomatic resources away from a measurable, enforceable output. In this case, as in others, the construct of climate policy obscures action on more defined issues.

U.S. President Donald Trump holds an executive order announcing the U.S. withdrawal from the Paris Agreement in Washington, D.C., on Jan. 20, 2025. Jim Watson/AFP via Getty Images)

This is about more than mere semantics. Retiring the prevailing definition of climate policy could enable the Democratic Party to pursue a fundamentally new approach to climate change mitigation and adaptation, one better suited to the nature of the federal government. Rather than centralizing decision-making in the White House, the next Democratic administration should empower the experts at federal agencies to use the tools at their disposal to reduce emissions.

Incidentally, the Paris Agreement offers a useful template for how this approach could work. The genius of this framework was in its acknowledgement of its own limitations. Realizing that few countries would agree to rules set by an external authority, the authors allowed each signatory to propose their own emission reduction plans-based on what their governments perceived to be achievable-and exposed them to public scrutiny to elevate their ambition.

A similar arrangement for the federal government would see each agency wield its own policy tools and expertise for progress on climate. Federal agencies would submit to the White House a plan for addressing climate change within their particular domain, along with a measurable emissions reduction target for the ensuing years. Each agency would rely on its own experts- with their years of experience in that domain-to assess where emissions reductions can be achieved and what levers that agency might pull to realize them.

The role of the White House would be more limited than under previous administrations, but nevertheless important. The Climate Policy Office (or its successor) would be charged with accepting and evaluating agencies' climate plans. White House officials would operate more as accountants than as policymakers, tracking each agency's progress against its targets. Certain cross-cutting initiatives would still likely require White House coordination.

But whenever possible, the next administration should resist the urge to roll out splashy, top-down programs, instead allowing climate considerations to quietly permeate the regular operations of federal agencies. Decentralizing and diffusing climate action into the federal bureaucracy would make it more targeted and likely to last. It would acknowledge that though most Americans want the federal government to tackle climate change, fewer prioritize it over public safety, the cost of living, or the economy. Climate action embedded into agency policymaking would reframe it as complementary to other objectives, not a competing priority.

All this would make rolling back climate progress a more complicated task for later administrations. In Trump's second term, the White House moved quickly to rescind Biden's climate directives, such as the Executive Order on Catalyzing Clean Energy Industries and Jobs Through Federal Sustainability, which committed the federal government to procuring clean energy. More distributed climate initiatives, particularly those lacking an explicit climate rationale, would require far more time and effort to identify and eliminate. Energy procurement plans at each federal agency implemented without the direction of an executive order would become at least more challenging to roll back than with the stroke of a president's pen.

This approach certainly comes with risk. It demands trust in civil servants and would require a substantial rehiring effort, with so many federal employees already in exodus. It may also fuel a perception that a future administration is not prioritizing climate action, which could come with political blowback. But the climate-concerned public has already grown frustrated with agendas that over-promise and under-deliver; a quiet yet effective approach is exactly what is needed. With discipline and sustained commitment, each of these risks is manageable-and well worth the potential upsides to emissions reduction and climate resilience.

If Democrats retake the White House in the 2028 elections, they will have a once-in-a-generation opportunity to rebuild the U.S. government's climate apparatus from the ground up. To capitalize on this opportunity, policy leaders must begin by interrogating the basic premise of climate policy that has undergirded previous platforms-and that process must begin today.

+ BOTTOM STOCK RANKED 1 FIRSTENERGY (FE: -$6.17 [-12.2%] VOL INDEX 1.3 [1 IS AVG]).

+ FirstEnergy Corp. (FE: $44.33) Market Cap $26 billion, Conventional electricity sector, P/E: 25.1, PV$1000 (20 yrs): $2,211

Past Month: Monthly: FirstEnergy Corp. (NYSE:FE) sinks 12%, leads Conventional Electricity sector

FirstEnergy Corp. (NYSE:FE $44.33), the NYSE's 11th largest Conventional Electricity company by market cap, has sunk $6.17 (or 12.2%) in the month ended May 31 to close at $44.33. This loss makes the stock the biggest decliner in the Conventional Electricity sector which is down 4.8%. Compared with the S&P 500 Index which rose 616.1 points (or 9.1%) in the month, this represented a relative price change of -21.3%.

The volume in the month was 1.3 times the average trading of 98.8 million shares per month.

Activities

FirstEnergy Corp., based in Akron, Ohio, is a leading electric utility holding company in the United States, serving millions of customers across the Midwest and Mid-Atlantic regions. Its core business revolves around the generation, transmission, and distribution of electricity through a diversified portfolio of power plants, including nuclear, natural gas, coal, and renewables. The company plays a vital role in maintaining reliable energy infrastructure, supporting economic growth, and advancing environmental stewardship. Among its fastest-growing segments are renewable energy initiatives, such as wind and solar projects, driven by increasing demand for clean power and regulatory incentives. Additionally, FirstEnergy is expanding in grid modernization and smart technology solutions, which enhance operational efficiency and integrate emerging technologies like energy storage and electric vehicle charging infrastructure. Committed to sustainable practices, the company invests in infrastructure upgrades to ensure long-term energy reliability and resilience. It is the NYSE's 11th largest Conventional electricity company by market capitalisation.

FirstEnergy Corp. (NYSE:FE) stands as a prominent electric utility with a strategic focus on robust power distribution and advanced transmission services. FirstEnergy power infrastructure underscores its dominance with cutting-edge outage restoration and smart grid solutions, while grid cybersecurity and the leadership of chief security officer Brian Harrell fortify FirstEnergy security operations center. Notably, FirstEnergy renewable energy programs and energy efficiency initiatives echo its commitment to sustainable growth. Catering to diverse clientele through reliable residential electric service and commercial energy solutions, FirstEnergy customer service elevates the consumer experience. Investment in FirstEnergy asset management and compliance with risk management reinforce financial and operational stability. Moreover, FirstEnergy demand response programs and substation maintenance showcase proactive emergency preparedness strategies, supported by comprehensive storm response and corporate security measures. Emphasizing strong investor relations, evidenced by active regulatory filings, FirstEnergy stock (FE) reflects adept navigation of industry dynamics and promising growth outlook.

+ BOTTOM STOCK RANKED 2 RTX (RTX: -$22.31 [-11.2%]).

Short Stock Dashboard

+ RTX Corporation (RTX: $176.09) Market Cap $236 billion, Aerospace sector, P/E: 35.1, PV$1000 (20 yrs): $4,502

Past Month: Monthly: RTX Corporation (NYSE:RTX) sinks 11%, leads Aerospace sector

RTX Corporation (NYSE:RTX $176.09), the NYSE's largest Aerospace company by market cap, has sunk $22.31 (or 11.2%) in the month ended May 31 to close at $176.09. This loss makes the stock the biggest decliner in the Aerospace sector which is down 3.5%. Compared with the S&P 500 Index which rose 616.1 points (or 9.1%) in the month, this represented a relative price change of -20.3%.

The volume in the month was 0.9 times the average trading of 100.8 million shares per month.

Activities

RTX Corporation, formerly known as Raytheon Technologies, is a global leader in aerospace and defense, formed through the 2020 merger of United Technologies and Raytheon. The company specializes in designing, manufacturing, and servicing advanced technologies, including commercial and military aircraft engines, missile systems, radar, cybersecurity solutions, and building automation. Its core business activities span three primary segments: Collins Aerospace, which provides avionics and interiors for both commercial and defense applications; Pratt & Whitney, focusing on jet engines and propulsion systems; and Raytheon, delivering integrated defense systems and intelligence solutions. Among these, the fastest-growing segments include commercial aerospace, driven by the recovery in air travel and demand for fuel-efficient engines, as well as defense electronics and cybersecurity, fueled by escalating global threats and digital transformation initiatives. RTX's innovation in sustainable aviation and advanced weaponry positions it as a key player in addressing evolving industry challenges, supporting governments and commercial clients worldwide. It is the NYSE's largest Aerospace company by market capitalisation.

RTX Corporation (NYSE:RTX) specializes in aerospace and defense solutions, leveraging technologies such as the Pratt & Whitney PW1000G Geared Turbofan for commercial aviation. Its portfolio includes Pratt & Whitney commercial aircraft engines and Pratt & Whitney military aircraft engines (F135), including the notable F135 fighter engine. RTX's service offerings encompass Aircraft engine MRO and aftermarket services. Collins Aerospace, an integral division, delivers Collins Aerospace avionics systems, Collins Aerospace flight control systems, and Collins Aerospace aircraft interiors and seating. In addition, RTX manufactures Auxiliary power units (APUs), environmental control systems, Landing gear and actuation systems, and groundbreaking Radar systems and airborne radars. The company excels in Airborne surveillance and ISR sensors, EO/IR targeting and imaging sensors, and develops the Patriot PAC-3 missile system, SM-6 Standard Missile, and Tomahawk cruise missile, alongside surface-to-air guided missiles and precision-guided munitions and strike weapons. Furthermore, RTX advances electronic warfare systems and countermeasures, hypersonic weapon systems and defenses, directed energy and laser weapons, while also producing satellite payloads, space sensors, spacecraft components, bus subsystems, secure satellite communications (satcom), cybersecurity and intelligence solutions, C4ISR and integrated battle management systems, flight deck displays and glass cockpits, turbofan engine components, spare parts, aircraft propulsion systems and turbomachinery, and commercial aerospace OEM systems and components.

Past Month: Monthly: Smith (A.O.) Corporation (NYSE:AOS) sinks 9%, leads Building Materials & Fixtures sector

Smith (A.O.) Corporation (NYSE:AOS $58.60), the NYSE's 13th largest Building Materials & Fixtures company by market cap, has sunk $5.71 (or 8.9%) in the month ended May 31 to close at $58.60. This loss makes the stock the biggest decliner in the Building Materials & Fixtures sector which is down 1.5%. Compared with the S&P 500 Index which rose 616.1 points (or 9.1%) in the month, this represented a relative price change of -18%.

The volume in the month was 1.2 times the average trading of 30.5 million shares per month.

Activities

A.O. Smith Corporation, a global leader in water technology, is headquartered in the United States and listed on the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE). The company specializes in designing, manufacturing, and marketing a wide range of water heating and treatment solutions, serving residential, commercial, and industrial markets. Its core business activities include producing high-efficiency water heaters, boilers, and advanced water purification systems, with a strong emphasis on innovation to address global water quality and conservation needs. In recent years, the fastest-growing segments have been its water treatment and softeners divisions, driven by increasing demand for clean water solutions in emerging markets like China and India, as well as the rising adoption of energy-efficient products in North America and Europe. This growth is supported by strategic investments in research and development, expanding the company's global footprint and enhancing its competitive edge in sustainable water technologies. Overall, A.O. Smith remains committed to delivering reliable, eco-friendly products that meet evolving customer demands while maintaining strong operational efficiency. It is the NYSE's 13th largest Building materials & fixtures company by market capitalisation.

Past Month: Monthly: Tapestry, Inc. (NYSE:TPR) decreases 7%, leads Clothing & Accessories sector

Tapestry, Inc. (NYSE:TPR $133.48), the NYSE's largest Clothing & Accessories company by market cap, has decreased $10.22 (or 7.1%) in the month ended May 31 to close at $133.48. This loss makes the stock the biggest decliner in the Clothing & Accessories sector which is down 4.4%. Compared with the S&P 500 Index which rose 616.1 points (or 9.1%) in the month, this represented a relative price change of -16.2%.

The volume in the month was 0.6 times the average trading of 66.6 million shares per month.

Activities

Tapestry, Inc., a New York-based company listed on the New York Stock Exchange, is a leading global luxury fashion house specializing in the design, marketing, distribution, and sale of high-quality handbags, accessories, footwear, and apparel. Through its portfolio of renowned brands, including Coach, Kate Spade New York, and Stuart Weitzman, Tapestry creates aspirational products that cater to diverse consumer preferences, emphasizing innovation, craftsmanship, and timeless style. The company's core business activities revolve around omnichannel retailing, which integrates physical stores, e-commerce, and wholesale channels to enhance customer engagement. In recent years, Tapestry's fastest-growing segments have included digital sales and e-commerce platforms, which have expanded rapidly due to increased online shopping trends, as well as its presence in emerging markets like Asia-Pacific. These areas are supported by strategic investments in digital transformation and product diversification, driving overall revenue growth and solidifying Tapestry's position in the competitive luxury goods industry. It is the NYSE's largest Clothing & accessories company by market capitalisation.

Tapestry, Inc. (NYSE:TPR) is a leading house of modern luxury accessories and lifestyle brands, known for its renowned labels such as Coach, Kate Spade, and Stuart Weitzman. Focused on its strong business model, Tapestry leverages a diversified portfolio encompassing Coach handbags, Coach leather goods, and Kate Spade accessories, effectively catering to the demands of luxury and fashion enthusiasts. With a strategic emphasis on Digital-first retail Tapestry and Mobile shopping app Tapestry, the company enhances its online presence, aligning with Gen Z fashion trends and the evolving social commerce fashion landscape. Notably, Tapestry's firm steps into sustainable leather goods have fortified its market position, while integrating Visual search shopping and Shoppable Pins Pinterest shopping to refine consumer experience. The integration of Augmented reality try-on shoes and Influencer marketing handbags highlights Tapestry's commitment to innovation. Strategically positioned, Tapestry's potential for growth remains formidable, supported by its omnichannel retail handbags approach.

Past Month: Monthly: Pentair plc. (NYSE:PNR) down 11% in past month, lowest amongst Industrial Machinery sector

Pentair plc. (NYSE:PNR $76.82), the NYSE's 12th largest Industrial Machinery company by market cap, has sunk $9.41 (or 10.9%) in the month ended May 31 to close at $76.82. This loss makes the stock the biggest decliner in the Industrial Machinery sector which is up 4.9%. Compared with the S&P 500 Index which rose 616.1 points (or 9.1%) in the month, this represented a relative price change of -20%.

The volume in the month was 1.4 times the average trading of 32.4 million shares per month.

Activities

Pentair plc, a global leader in water solutions headquartered in the United States and listed on the New York Stock Exchange, specializes in designing, manufacturing, and marketing innovative products and services for water treatment, filtration, pumps, and valves. The company's core business activities span three main segments: Flow Technologies, which focuses on fluid handling solutions; Water Solutions, encompassing residential and commercial water purification systems; and Pool, which provides equipment for swimming pools and spas. Among these, the Water Solutions segment has emerged as the fastest-growing, driven by increasing demand for sustainable water management technologies amid global water scarcity challenges. Pentair's commitment to efficiency and environmental stewardship has fueled its expansion into smart, connected devices and advanced filtration systems, supporting diverse industries such as residential, industrial, and infrastructure. This strategic focus positions Pentair as a key player in addressing critical water needs worldwide. It is the NYSE's 12th largest Industrial machinery company by market capitalisation.

Pentair plc (NYSE:PNR) is a leading provider of water management solutions, strategically positioned in the industry through its comprehensive portfolio, including Pentair pool pumps and Pentair water filtration systems. With innovation at its core, the firm offers top-tier products like Pentair reverse osmosis systems and Pentair Everpure filters, catering to residential, commercial, and industrial applications. Known for pioneering energy efficiency with products such as the Pentair IntelliFlo variable speed pump and Pentair variable speed pool pumps, Pentair addresses modern demands for sustainable solutions. Its diverse offerings extend to Pentair pool automation systems and commercial water treatment systems, underlining its commitment to technological advancement and customer satisfaction. As a trusted partner in water treatment, Pentair’s market reach spans Pentair water filtration replacement cartridges and Pentair wastewater treatment equipment, positioning it for continued growth across municipal, industrial, and residential sectors, harnessing evolving trends in water sustainability and management.

Activities

FirstEnergy Corp., based in Akron, Ohio, is a leading electric utility holding company in the United States, serving millions of customers across the Midwest and Mid-Atlantic regions. Its core business revolves around the generation, transmission, and distribution of electricity through a diversified portfolio of power plants, including nuclear, natural gas, coal, and renewables. The company plays a vital role in maintaining reliable energy infrastructure, supporting economic growth, and advancing environmental stewardship. Among its fastest-growing segments are renewable energy initiatives, such as wind and solar projects, driven by increasing demand for clean power and regulatory incentives. Additionally, FirstEnergy is expanding in grid modernization and smart technology solutions, which enhance operational efficiency and integrate emerging technologies like energy storage and electric vehicle charging infrastructure. Committed to sustainable practices, the company invests in infrastructure upgrades to ensure long-term energy reliability and resilience. It is the NYSE's 11th largest Conventional electricity company by market capitalisation.

FirstEnergy Corp. (NYSE:FE) stands as a prominent electric utility with a strategic focus on robust power distribution and advanced transmission services. FirstEnergy power infrastructure underscores its dominance with cutting-edge outage restoration and smart grid solutions, while grid cybersecurity and the leadership of chief security officer Brian Harrell fortify FirstEnergy security operations center. Notably, FirstEnergy renewable energy programs and energy efficiency initiatives echo its commitment to sustainable growth. Catering to diverse clientele through reliable residential electric service and commercial energy solutions, FirstEnergy customer service elevates the consumer experience. Investment in FirstEnergy asset management and compliance with risk management reinforce financial and operational stability. Moreover, FirstEnergy demand response programs and substation maintenance showcase proactive emergency preparedness strategies, supported by comprehensive storm response and corporate security measures. Emphasizing strong investor relations, evidenced by active regulatory filings, FirstEnergy stock (FE) reflects adept navigation of industry dynamics and promising growth outlook.

CMS Energy Corporation (NYSE:CMS $74.05), the NYSE's 13th largest Conventional electricity company by market cap, extended its slide in the past week ended Wednesday, shedding an additional 68.0c (or 0.9%) to close at $74.05. The shares have plummeted $4.77 (or 6.1%) over the past four weeks.

Activities

CMS Energy Corporation, a New York Stock Exchange-listed utility holding company headquartered in Jackson, Michigan, focuses on providing reliable energy solutions across the United States. Its core business activities center on electricity generation, transmission, and distribution, primarily through its subsidiary Consumers Energy, which serves over 6.7 million of Michigan's residents with electric and natural gas services. The company also engages in infrastructure development, including natural gas pipelines and storage, while advancing energy efficiency programs. Among its fastest-growing segments are renewable energy initiatives, such as solar and wind projects, driven by increasing demand for clean power and regulatory support for decarbonization efforts. These efforts have fueled expansion in electric vehicle infrastructure and grid modernization, positioning CMS Energy for sustained growth in a transitioning energy landscape. With a commitment to sustainable practices, the company continues to invest in innovative technologies to enhance reliability and reduce environmental impact. It is the NYSE's 13th largest Conventional electricity company by market capitalisation.

CMS Energy stock, specifically CMS Energy NYSE:CMS, presents an intriguing investment opportunity for individuals looking to diversify with a regulated utility stock CMS. The CMS Energy dividend yield offers a stable return, appealing to long-term investors. With the recent Consumers Energy $276.6 million rate hike, CMS Energy aims to accommodate its Consumers Energy electric service and Consumers Energy natural gas service improvements. The approved Consumers Energy rate case allows for Michigan utility rate hike, impacting Consumers Energy customers bills beginning in May.

CMS Energy earnings reflect a robust financial health, supported by their commitment to CMS Energy renewable energy projects. The strategic Consumers Energy clean energy initiatives and CMS Energy grid modernization efforts are pivotal to CMS Energy growth prospects. Additionally, Consumers Energy infrastructure investments focus on CMS Energy sustainability, reinforcing its position as a leading utility dividend stock Michigan.

With Consumers Energy energy efficiency programs and Consumers Energy power generation advancements, CMS Energy regulated operations align with emergent market trends. Despite the Michigan electricity rates increase, Consumers Energy bill relief programs aim to mitigate the impact on customers. CMS Energy financials show a promising CMS Energy market outlook, emphasizing its dedication to long-term growth and innovation.

Activities

FirstEnergy Corp., based in Akron, Ohio, is a leading electric utility holding company in the United States, serving millions of customers across the Midwest and Mid-Atlantic regions. Its core business revolves around the generation, transmission, and distribution of electricity through a diversified portfolio of power plants, including nuclear, natural gas, coal, and renewables. The company plays a vital role in maintaining reliable energy infrastructure, supporting economic growth, and advancing environmental stewardship. Among its fastest-growing segments are renewable energy initiatives, such as wind and solar projects, driven by increasing demand for clean power and regulatory incentives. Additionally, FirstEnergy is expanding in grid modernization and smart technology solutions, which enhance operational efficiency and integrate emerging technologies like energy storage and electric vehicle charging infrastructure. Committed to sustainable practices, the company invests in infrastructure upgrades to ensure long-term energy reliability and resilience. It is the NYSE's 11th largest Conventional electricity company by market capitalisation.

FirstEnergy Corp. (NYSE:FE) stands as a prominent electric utility with a strategic focus on robust power distribution and advanced transmission services. FirstEnergy power infrastructure underscores its dominance with cutting-edge outage restoration and smart grid solutions, while grid cybersecurity and the leadership of chief security officer Brian Harrell fortify FirstEnergy security operations center. Notably, FirstEnergy renewable energy programs and energy efficiency initiatives echo its commitment to sustainable growth. Catering to diverse clientele through reliable residential electric service and commercial energy solutions, FirstEnergy customer service elevates the consumer experience. Investment in FirstEnergy asset management and compliance with risk management reinforce financial and operational stability. Moreover, FirstEnergy demand response programs and substation maintenance showcase proactive emergency preparedness strategies, supported by comprehensive storm response and corporate security measures. Emphasizing strong investor relations, evidenced by active regulatory filings, FirstEnergy stock (FE) reflects adept navigation of industry dynamics and promising growth outlook.

SECTION 2 CORPORATE PROFILE

2.1 Activities

RPM International Inc., a multinational company listed on the New York Stock Exchange, specializes in the manufacture and marketing of high-performance coatings, sealants, adhesives, and building materials. Headquartered in Ohio, USA, the company operates through subsidiaries like Rust-Oleum and DAP, serving industrial, construction, and consumer markets with innovative solutions for corrosion protection, waterproofing, and infrastructure maintenance. Its core activities focus on developing durable, sustainable products that enhance performance in challenging environments, including paints, roofing systems, and specialty chemicals. Among its fastest-growing segments are performance coatings for energy and infrastructure sectors, driven by rising demand for advanced materials in renewable energy projects and urban development, as well as consumer DIY products fueled by home improvement trends. This strategic emphasis on innovation and expansion has positioned RPM as a key player in the global specialty materials industry, with a commitment to quality and environmental stewardship. It is the NYSE's 9th largest Chemicals company by market capitalisation.

SECTION 3 PAST YEAR PRESS RELEASES

Press Release article 1 of 5, Source: WEC, 77 words

April 03: RPM International quarterly dividends steady

RPM International today announced a quarterly dividend of 54.0c per share for Q3/2026; unchanged from the previous quarter (Q2/2026) dividend of 54.0c. The ex-dividend date is Thursday, April 16, 2026 and the record date is Thursday, April 16, 2026 and it is payable on Thursday, April 30. Total dividends per share paid in the 12 months ended April 03 were $2.10.

Press Release article 2 of 5, Source: Company Website, 299 words

January 08: RPM Reports Fiscal 2026 Second Quarter Results

- Record second-quarter sales of $1.91 billion, an increase of 3.5% compared to the prior-year record

- Second-quarter net income of $161.2 million, diluted EPS of $1.26, and EBIT of $229.0 million

- Second-quarter adjusted diluted EPS of $1.20, a decrease of 13.7% compared to the prior-year record and adjusted EBIT of $226.6 million, a decrease of 11.2% compared to the prior-year record

- Fiscal 2026 third-quarter outlook calls for mid-single-digit sales growth and adjusted EBIT to increase mid- to high-single digits

- Fiscal 2026 fourth-quarter outlook calls for mid-single-digit sales growth and adjusted EBIT to increase low- to high-single-digits

- Implementing SG&A-focused optimization actions that are expected to generate benefits of approximately $100 million annually

MEDINA, Ohio--(BUSINESS WIRE)-- RPM International Inc. (NYSE: RPM), a world leader in specialty coatings, sealants and building materials, today reported financial results for its fiscal 2026 second quarter ended November 30, 2025.

Frank C. Sullivan, RPM chairman and CEO commented, "In the second quarter, sales came in at the lower end of our expectations. The prolonged government shutdown contributed to the trend of longer lead times on construction projects and further pressured already negative consumer sentiment. As a result, sales growth turned negative as the quarter progressed, and earnings declined as we were unable to fully leverage growth investments and overcome temporary margin headwinds from plant and warehouse facility consolidations. Given the slower demand environment, we have moved quickly to put in place SG&A-focused optimization actions that will save approximately $100 million annually once fully implemented, while continuing focused growth investments in our highest potential opportunities."

SG&A-Focused Optimization Actions

In response to current market conditions, the company is implementing actions that, once fully in place, will generate annual benefits of approximately $100 million.

Press Release article 3 of 5, Source: Company Website, 260 words

January 07: RPM Announces Acquisition of Kalzip

Acquisition expands RPM's global building envelope portfolio

MEDINA, Ohio--(BUSINESS WIRE)-- RPM International Inc. (NYSE:RPM) today announced that a definitive agreement has been signed for its Tremco Construction Products Group ("Tremco CPG") to acquire Kalzip GmbH ("Kalzip"), a global leader in the design and production of metal-based roofs and facades for building envelopes. The transaction will bolster Tremco CPG's building envelope systems capabilities with a premier brand known for architectural innovation and high-performance solutions that meet demanding specifications.

Kalzip is a globally recognized leader in aluminum roofing and facade systems. Headquartered in Koblenz, Germany, the company specializes in customized lightweight, weather-resistant building envelope technologies. Delivering over 1.2 billion square feet of aluminum solutions worldwide, key projects have included El Prat Airport roof refurbishment in Barcelona, Mercedes Benz Arena in Berlin, Children's Hospital Dublin and Ikea Dubai. Kalzip generated calendar year 2024 net sales of approximately EUR75 million. The acquisition enhances Tremco CPG's position in key international markets, including Europe and India, while creating future opportunities to expand Kalzip's aluminum systems to North American customers.

The addition of Kalzip represents a strategic enhancement to Tremco CPG's integrated portfolio of building envelope solutions, which includes roofing restoration and maintenance systems, field-applied and panelized EIFS, panel systems, sealants, waterproofing and more. This combination aligns with Tremco CPG's strategy to offer a seamless, end-to-end approach to construction that enhances building efficiency, durability and aesthetics.

The transaction is expected to close in the fourth quarter of fiscal 2026, subject to customary closing conditions.

December 05 2025: RPM to Announce Fiscal 2026 Second-Quarter Results on January 8, 2026

MEDINA, Ohio--(BUSINESS WIRE)-- RPM International Inc. (NYSE: RPM) announced today that it will release its financial results for the fiscal 2026 second quarter before the stock market opens on Thursday, January 8, 2026. The results will be issued via newswire and will also be available on the RPM website at www.RPMinc.com.

Management will host a conference call to discuss the results beginning at 10:00 a.m. Eastern Time the same day. The call can be accessed via webcast at www.RPMinc.com/Investors/Presentations-Webcasts/ or by dialing 844-481-2915, or 412-317-0708 for international callers. Participants are asked to call the assigned number approximately 10 minutes before the conference call begins and request to join the RPM International call. The call, which will last approximately one hour, will be open to the public, but only financial analysts will be permitted to ask questions. The media and all other participants will be in a listen-only mode.

For those unable to listen to the live call, a replay will be available from January 8, 2026, until January 15, 2026. The replay can be accessed by dialing 855-669-9658, or 412-317-0088 for international callers. The access code is 1320592. The call also will be available both live and for replay, and as a written transcript, via the RPM website at www.RPMinc.com.

Fri Apr 24, 2026: Duke Energy Secures 20-Year License for Robinson Nuclear Plant

Duke Energy Corporation has received a significant boost with the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission's (NRC) decision to grant a 20-year extension for the operating license of its Robinson Nuclear Plant. This renewal allows the facility to continue producing energy until the year 2050, a crucial development for the utility giant. The announcement was made on April 23rd, 2026, and signifies Duke Energy's commitment to providing stable and sustainable energy to the region.

The Robinson Nuclear Plant, located in Hartsville, South Carolina, plays a vital role in Duke Energy's operations, supplying electricity to approximately 570,000 households. This license extension not only secures the plant's operational future but also aligns with Duke Energy's broader strategy of maintaining a diverse energy portfolio. The company is focused on integrating nuclear, natural gas, and renewable energy sources to meet the growing energy demands of its 8 million customers across various states, including the Carolinas, Indiana, Florida, Ohio, and Kentucky.

Duke Energy operates within the regulated utilities sector, boasting a market capitalization of approximately $99.64 billion. The company is segmented into electric utilities and infrastructure, gas utilities and infrastructure, and commercial renewables. Currently, the company's P/E ratio stands at 20.26, which is close to its five-year median, indicating that the stock may be fairly valued in the current market context. Investors are encouraged to consider this valuation alongside other financial metrics when evaluating the stock's potential.

The GF Score(TradeMark) for Duke Energy is 77 out of 100, reflecting strong performance across key metrics that can lead to higher long-term returns. The company's profitability rank is notably strong at 7 out of 10, underscoring its operational efficiency. However, concerns regarding financial strength persist, with a rating of just 3 out of 10, suggesting potential issues related to debt levels and overall financial health. Investors should carefully weigh these factors when considering Duke Energy as part of their investment portfolio.

In recent months, insider trading activity has raised eyebrows, with insiders selling $5.6 million worth of shares and no recorded insider buying. This trend may lead to questions about insider confidence in the company's future performance. Overall, Duke Energy presents a mixed outlook for investors. While the recent license renewal for the Robinson Nuclear Plant strengthens its operational foundation, its financial challenges could impact long-term growth. Investors are encouraged to explore further insights about Duke Energy and its current market position.

Recent News 2 of 3, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 395 words

Wed Apr 22, 2026: Duke Energy Foundation Launches America250 Grants for South Carolina Green Spaces

GREENVILLE, S.C.– To strengthen communities across South Carolina as the nation approaches its 250th anniversary, Duke Energy Foundation is awarding $275,000 to nonprofit organizations through a first round of funding as part of its America250 initiative. This funding, announced on 22nd April 2026, celebrates Earth Day and aims to enhance local parks, tree planting efforts, and trail maintenance projects throughout the Palmetto State.

The grants, which form part of a total investment exceeding $550,000 in South Carolina, are aimed at revitalizing parks and shared green spaces, supporting tree planting, and litter prevention initiatives. "As we look back on our country's 250-year anniversary, it's an opportunity to reflect on our nation's history while working to support the next generation," said Tim Pearson, Duke Energy South Carolina president. "We are proud to partner with local organizations who can help care for the Palmetto State now, so future generations can enjoy its beauty."

Duke Energy Foundation has awarded a total of 11 grants in this first round of funding. Notable recipients include the South Carolina Wildlife Federation, Foothills Trail Conservancy, and the Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance. Each organization is expected to utilize the funding to enhance local environments and foster community engagement in conservation efforts. For instance, Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance plans to create a new trail that highlights the scenic beauty of Lake Keowee while educating visitors about the Revolutionary War history in the area.

The positive responses from grant recipients highlight the importance of such initiatives. Matt Flege, Executive Director of Upstate Greenways & Trails Alliance, expressed gratitude for the support, stating, "The America250 grant will allow us to create a new trail showcasing the beauty of Lake Keowee to South Carolina residents and visitors alike." Similarly, Susan H. Wild, Chair of Sumter Green, emphasized how the investment strengthens their commitment to beautifying the community, ensuring that efforts made today will benefit future generations.

These grants mark the first awards under Duke Energy Foundation's America250 initiative, which represents a commitment of over $1 million towards community-driven projects across the company's six-state service territories. Additional grant recipients will be announced later this spring, including initiatives focused on veterans' workforce pathways and enhancing access to history and civics education. Duke Energy Foundation continues to provide nearly $30 million annually in philanthropic support to the communities it serves, reflecting its dedication to environmental stewardship and community development.

Recent News 3 of 3, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 271 words

Wed Apr 22, 2026: Duke Energy Honored with Arbor Day Foundation’s Tree Line USA Award for 20th Year

Duke Energy Corporation has received national recognition for its tree care practices for the 20th consecutive year. The Arbor Day Foundation named Duke Energy Florida a 'Tree Line USA' utility, citing the company's rigorous tree pruning standards, comprehensive employee training, and public education efforts aimed at reducing power outages and safeguarding electrical infrastructure.

On 22nd April 2026, the Arbor Day Foundation lauded Duke Energy for meeting industry standards for tree care, supporting tree planting initiatives, and engaging in annual arbor education programs. The designation reflects Duke Energy's commitment to sustainable practices, as well as its proactive approach to maintaining the health of urban trees in proximity to electrical lines.

One of Duke Energy's ongoing initiatives includes an annual free tree giveaway for customers through the Arbor Day Foundation's Energy-Saving Trees program. In 2026, the company distributed 1,000 trees to Florida customers, bringing the total number of free trees given away statewide to over 16,000 since 2017. These trees are shipped directly to customers with detailed planting instructions designed to encourage proper placement away from power lines.

Company officials noted that proper tree placement is essential for conserving energy and minimizing the risk of outages during storm and hurricane seasons. Duke Energy also conducts year-round vegetation maintenance near power lines and ramps up these efforts ahead of hurricane season, ensuring a reliable power supply for its customers.

Additionally, Duke Energy reminds customers not to trim trees near power lines themselves and encourages them to contact Sunshine 811 before digging near underground utilities. More information about responsible tree planting near electrical equipment can be found through Duke Energy's Right Tree Right Place program.

value.Title: Institute for Energy Economics & Financial Analysis: 'Germany's Gas Dependence: An Energy Security Risk'

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK7-DKF3-RX82-91Y7-00000-00Institute for Energy Economics & Financial Analysis: 'Germany's Gas Dependence: An Energy Security Risk'2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:08:55ZLexisNexisInstitute for Energy Economics & Financial Analysis: 'Germany's Gas Dependence: An Energy Security Risk'Targeted News ServiceLAKEWOOD, Ohio

(TNSLrpt) -- The Institute for Energy Economics and Financial Analysis issued the following report on March 31, 2026 by Ana Maria Jaller-Makarewicz, Kevin Leung, Jonathan Bruegel and Andrew Reid entitled "Germany's gas dependence: An energy security risk."

Here are excerpts:

* * *

Key Findings

Germany's residential heat pump installations from 2022 to 2025 saved the country Euros1.3 billion on liquefied natural gas imports in the three years between 2023 and 2025.

Germany should continue replacing gas demand with renewables, heat pumps and energy efficiency programmes. This would lower the country's exposure to volatile gas prices and strengthen its energy security.

Germany is betting on hydrogen power to support renewables. But high costs, infrastructure gaps and project execution risks suggest hydrogen power will play a smaller role than government forecasts.

High costs and technical risks mean carbon capture is not a near-term solution for Germany's planned 10 gigawatts of new gas power plants. Utilities relying on carbon capture face uncertainty over emissions cuts.

Executive Summary

Germany can lower its exposure to volatile gas prices and geopolitical conflicts by scaling up renewable energy and clean heating systems. These, alongside cross-border grid connections and energy efficiency programmes, would displace gas generation and reduce the need for expensive hydrogen infrastructure. IEEFA estimates that this strategy would allow Germany to source just 5% of its electricity from natural gas and hydrogen combined by 2045.

The country has already made significant progress. Its gas consumption peaked in 2021. Electrification and the installation of heat pumps contributed to a 23% decline in household gas use between 2021 and 2024. IEEFA estimates that the almost 1.1 million residential heat pumps that Germany installed from 2022 to 2025 saved the country Euros1.3 billion on liquefied natural gas (LNG) imports between 2023 and 2025.

Germany's LNG terminal utilisation rate in 2025 was 36.3%, far below the EU average. This indicates that decisions to build LNG terminals failed to fully consider future gas demand. Nonetheless, the country still plans to almost triple its LNG import capacity by the end of 2028.

In the power sector, Germany's government prioritises natural gas and hydrogen to support a system dominated by solar and wind. The country plans to build 10 gigawatts of hydrogen-ready gas power stations. But hydrogen power plants will likely play a smaller role than government plans because of their high expense and low efficiency.

Germany may aim to decarbonise some of its gas power stations with carbon capture and storage (CCS). But CCS has never been used at scale on existing gas plants because of technical and economic challenges. CCS projects globally have underperformed, costs are prohibitively high, and Germany does not have a carbon dioxide pipeline network.

CCS is therefore not a near-term solution for Germany's planned 10-gigawatt gas buildout, in IEEFA's view. If the country does attempt to decarbonise these assets with CCS, subsidies could cost hundreds of billions of euros. The gamble is that reliance on the technology could delay more viable alternatives and expose Germany to project failures. These same issues apply if Germany uses blue hydrogen (produced from natural gas with carbon capture) as a fuel in power plants.

German utilities planning to rely on CCS may also face uncertainty in reducing emissions. Despite aiming to decarbonise gas power plants, some utilities have recently signed contracts to import LNG for up to 20 years. These contractual obligations expose the companies to the risk of long-term underutilised gas power plants, potentially locking them into uneconomic supply commitments.

German utilities should carefully weigh the transition risks before signing any more long-term gas import contracts. This would help them avoid additional financial impacts related to the underutilisation of gas plants and align them more closely with the country's climate and energy security goals.

Given these findings, IEEFA recommends that Germany do the following:

* Continue replacing gas demand with renewables, electrification of heating and energy efficiency programmes

* Significantly scale up cross-border grid connections and demand-side management, which would help minimise gas and hydrogen generation

* Cancel plans to build new LNG import terminals, given that it has already passed peak gas consumption

* Carefully monitor gas infrastructure investments to prevent unnecessary increases in consumer gas prices

* Not rely on CCS to decarbonise gas power plants

These measures would provide Germany with a more sustainable and cost-effective path to energy security.

* * *

View full report at: https://ieefa.org/resources/germanys-gas-dependence-energy-security-risk

value.Title: Spartan Names Riley to Lead in South Texas
value.Overview: MAUMEE, Ohio -- Spartan Chemical Company , a manufacturer of sustainable cleaning and sanitation solutions for the industrial...

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJG-12G3-S15X-C02Y-00000-00Spartan Names Riley to Lead in South Texas2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:08:55ZLexisNexisNEWSSpartan Names Riley to Lead in South Texas

MAUMEE, Ohio-- Spartan Chemical Company, a manufacturer of sustainable cleaning and sanitation solutions for the industrial...

MAUMEE, Ohio -- Spartan Chemical Company, a manufacturer of sustainable cleaning and sanitation solutions for the industrial and institutional market, has promoted Nelson Riley to regional manager for South Texas. For the past year, Riley has held the role...

MAUMEE, Ohio -- Spartan Chemical Company, a manufacturer of sustainable cleaning and sanitation solutions for the industrial and institutional market, has promoted Nelson Riley to regional manager for South Texas. For the past year, Riley has held the role of sales business development for Foxguard Solutions, where he was responsible for partnering with businesses to provide cutting-edge industrial computing solutions. Prior to his time with Foxguard Solutions, he worked for Texas Solar in San Antonio, Texas, for four years. During his time there, he held the position of sales manager, where he was responsible for leading a team of dynamic solar consultants to educate homeowners about the benefits of renewable energy. Finally, Mr. Riley worked for Lindsey Dental in San Antonio, Texas, for three years as the practice manager. In this role, he managed daily operations, marketing, and sales of multiple dental offices. Nelson attended West Texas A&M University, in Canyon, Texas where he received his master’s degree in business administration. For more, go to spartanchemical.com.

SECTION 2 CORPORATE PROFILE

2.1 Activities

RPM International Inc., a multinational company listed on the New York Stock Exchange, specializes in the manufacture and marketing of high-performance coatings, sealants, adhesives, and building materials. Headquartered in Ohio, USA, the company operates through subsidiaries like Rust-Oleum and DAP, serving industrial, construction, and consumer markets with innovative solutions for corrosion protection, waterproofing, and infrastructure maintenance. Its core activities focus on developing durable, sustainable products that enhance performance in challenging environments, including paints, roofing systems, and specialty chemicals. Among its fastest-growing segments are performance coatings for energy and infrastructure sectors, driven by rising demand for advanced materials in renewable energy projects and urban development, as well as consumer DIY products fueled by home improvement trends. This strategic emphasis on innovation and expansion has positioned RPM as a key player in the global specialty materials industry, with a commitment to quality and environmental stewardship. It is the NYSE's 9th largest Chemicals company by market capitalisation.

SECTION 3 PAST YEAR PRESS RELEASES

3.1 Press Releases and Corporate Wire

Press Release article 1 of 5, Source: WEC, 77 words

April 03: RPM International quarterly dividends steady

RPM International today announced a quarterly dividend of 54.0c per share for Q3/2026; unchanged from the previous quarter (Q2/2026) dividend of 54.0c. The ex-dividend date is Thursday, April 16, 2026 and the record date is Thursday, April 16, 2026 and it is payable on Thursday, April 30. Total dividends per share paid in the 12 months ended April 03 were $2.10.

Press Release article 2 of 5, Source: Company Website, 299 words

January 08: RPM Reports Fiscal 2026 Second Quarter Results

- Record second-quarter sales of $1.91 billion, an increase of 3.5% compared to the prior-year record

- Second-quarter net income of $161.2 million, diluted EPS of $1.26, and EBIT of $229.0 million

- Second-quarter adjusted diluted EPS of $1.20, a decrease of 13.7% compared to the prior-year record and adjusted EBIT of $226.6 million, a decrease of 11.2% compared to the prior-year record

- Fiscal 2026 third-quarter outlook calls for mid-single-digit sales growth and adjusted EBIT to increase mid- to high-single digits

- Fiscal 2026 fourth-quarter outlook calls for mid-single-digit sales growth and adjusted EBIT to increase low- to high-single-digits

- Implementing SG&A-focused optimization actions that are expected to generate benefits of approximately $100 million annually

MEDINA, Ohio--(BUSINESS WIRE)-- RPM International Inc. (NYSE: RPM), a world leader in specialty coatings, sealants and building materials, today reported financial results for its fiscal 2026 second quarter ended November 30, 2025.

Frank C. Sullivan, RPM chairman and CEO commented, "In the second quarter, sales came in at the lower end of our expectations. The prolonged government shutdown contributed to the trend of longer lead times on construction projects and further pressured already negative consumer sentiment. As a result, sales growth turned negative as the quarter progressed, and earnings declined as we were unable to fully leverage growth investments and overcome temporary margin headwinds from plant and warehouse facility consolidations. Given the slower demand environment, we have moved quickly to put in place SG&A-focused optimization actions that will save approximately $100 million annually once fully implemented, while continuing focused growth investments in our highest potential opportunities."

SG&A-Focused Optimization Actions

In response to current market conditions, the company is implementing actions that, once fully in place, will generate annual benefits of approximately $100 million.

Press Release article 3 of 5, Source: Company Website, 260 words

January 07: RPM Announces Acquisition of Kalzip

Acquisition expands RPM's global building envelope portfolio

MEDINA, Ohio--(BUSINESS WIRE)-- RPM International Inc. (NYSE:RPM) today announced that a definitive agreement has been signed for its Tremco Construction Products Group ("Tremco CPG") to acquire Kalzip GmbH ("Kalzip"), a global leader in the design and production of metal-based roofs and facades for building envelopes. The transaction will bolster Tremco CPG's building envelope systems capabilities with a premier brand known for architectural innovation and high-performance solutions that meet demanding specifications.

Kalzip is a globally recognized leader in aluminum roofing and facade systems. Headquartered in Koblenz, Germany, the company specializes in customized lightweight, weather-resistant building envelope technologies. Delivering over 1.2 billion square feet of aluminum solutions worldwide, key projects have included El Prat Airport roof refurbishment in Barcelona, Mercedes Benz Arena in Berlin, Children's Hospital Dublin and Ikea Dubai. Kalzip generated calendar year 2024 net sales of approximately EUR75 million. The acquisition enhances Tremco CPG's position in key international markets, including Europe and India, while creating future opportunities to expand Kalzip's aluminum systems to North American customers.

The addition of Kalzip represents a strategic enhancement to Tremco CPG's integrated portfolio of building envelope solutions, which includes roofing restoration and maintenance systems, field-applied and panelized EIFS, panel systems, sealants, waterproofing and more. This combination aligns with Tremco CPG's strategy to offer a seamless, end-to-end approach to construction that enhances building efficiency, durability and aesthetics.

The transaction is expected to close in the fourth quarter of fiscal 2026, subject to customary closing conditions.

Press Release article 4 of 5, Source: WEC, 77 words

RPM International today announced a quarterly dividend of 54.0c per share for Q2/2026; unchanged from the previous quarter (Q1/2026) dividend of 54.0c. The ex-dividend date is Friday, January 16, 2026 and the record date is Friday, January 16, 2026 and it is payable on Friday, January 30. December 05 2025: RPM to Announce Fiscal 2026 Second-Quarter Results on January 8, 2026

MEDINA, Ohio--(BUSINESS WIRE)-- RPM International Inc. (NYSE: RPM) announced today that it will release its financial results for the fiscal 2026 second quarter before the stock market opens on Thursday, January 8, 2026. The results will be issued via newswire and will also be available on the RPM website at www.RPMinc.com.

Management will host a conference call to discuss the results beginning at 10:00 a.m. Eastern Time the same day. The call can be accessed via webcast at www.RPMinc.com/Investors/Presentations-Webcasts/ or by dialing 844-481-2915, or 412-317-0708 for international callers. Participants are asked to call the assigned number approximately 10 minutes before the conference call begins and request to join the RPM International call. The call, which will last approximately one hour, will be open to the public, but only financial analysts will be permitted to ask questions. The media and all other participants will be in a listen-only mode.

For those unable to listen to the live call, a replay will be available from January 8, 2026, until January 15, 2026. The replay can be accessed by dialing 855-669-9658, or 412-317-0088 for international callers. The access code is 1320592. The call also will be available both live and for replay, and as a written transcript, via the RPM website at www.RPMinc.com.

SECTION 2 CORPORATE PROFILE

2.1 Activities

RPM International Inc., a multinational company listed on the New York Stock Exchange, specializes in the manufacture and marketing of high-performance coatings, sealants, adhesives, and building materials. Headquartered in Ohio, USA, the company operates through subsidiaries like Rust-Oleum and DAP, serving industrial, construction, and consumer markets with innovative solutions for corrosion protection, waterproofing, and infrastructure maintenance. Its core activities focus on developing durable, sustainable products that enhance performance in challenging environments, including paints, roofing systems, and specialty chemicals. Among its fastest-growing segments are performance coatings for energy and infrastructure sectors, driven by rising demand for advanced materials in renewable energy projects and urban development, as well as consumer DIY products fueled by home improvement trends. This strategic emphasis on innovation and expansion has positioned RPM as a key player in the global specialty materials industry, with a commitment to quality and environmental stewardship. It is the NYSE's 10th largest Chemicals company by market capitalisation.

Pentair plc (NYSE:PNR) is a leading provider of water management solutions, strategically positioned in the industry through its comprehensive portfolio, including Pentair pool pumps and Pentair water filtration systems. With innovation at its core, the firm offers top-tier products like Pentair reverse osmosis systems and Pentair Everpure filters, catering to residential, commercial, and industrial applications. Known for pioneering energy efficiency with products such as the Pentair IntelliFlo variable speed pump and Pentair variable speed pool pumps, Pentair addresses modern demands for sustainable solutions. Its diverse offerings extend to Pentair pool automation systems and commercial water treatment systems, underlining its commitment to technological advancement and customer satisfaction. As a trusted partner in water treatment, Pentair’s market reach spans Pentair water filtration replacement cartridges and Pentair wastewater treatment equipment, positioning it for continued growth across municipal, industrial, and residential sectors, harnessing evolving trends in water sustainability and management.

SECTION 3 PAST YEAR PRESS RELEASES

3.1 Press Releases and Corporate Wire

Press Release article 1 of 5, Source: WEC, 77 words

April 03: RPM International quarterly dividends steady

RPM International today announced a quarterly dividend of 54.0c per share for Q3/2026; unchanged from the previous quarter (Q2/2026) dividend of 54.0c. The ex-dividend date is Thursday, April 16, 2026 and the record date is Thursday, April 16, 2026 and it is payable on Thursday, April 30. Total dividends per share paid in the 12 months ended April 03 were $2.10.

Press Release article 2 of 5, Source: Company Website, 299 words

January 08: RPM Reports Fiscal 2026 Second Quarter Results

- Record second-quarter sales of $1.91 billion, an increase of 3.5% compared to the prior-year record

- Second-quarter net income of $161.2 million, diluted EPS of $1.26, and EBIT of $229.0 million

- Second-quarter adjusted diluted EPS of $1.20, a decrease of 13.7% compared to the prior-year record and adjusted EBIT of $226.6 million, a decrease of 11.2% compared to the prior-year record

- Fiscal 2026 third-quarter outlook calls for mid-single-digit sales growth and adjusted EBIT to increase mid- to high-single digits

- Fiscal 2026 fourth-quarter outlook calls for mid-single-digit sales growth and adjusted EBIT to increase low- to high-single-digits

- Implementing SG&A-focused optimization actions that are expected to generate benefits of approximately $100 million annually

MEDINA, Ohio--(BUSINESS WIRE)-- RPM International Inc. (NYSE: RPM), a world leader in specialty coatings, sealants and building materials, today reported financial results for its fiscal 2026 second quarter ended November 30, 2025.

Frank C. Sullivan, RPM chairman and CEO commented, "In the second quarter, sales came in at the lower end of our expectations. The prolonged government shutdown contributed to the trend of longer lead times on construction projects and further pressured already negative consumer sentiment. As a result, sales growth turned negative as the quarter progressed, and earnings declined as we were unable to fully leverage growth investments and overcome temporary margin headwinds from plant and warehouse facility consolidations. Given the slower demand environment, we have moved quickly to put in place SG&A-focused optimization actions that will save approximately $100 million annually once fully implemented, while continuing focused growth investments in our highest potential opportunities."

SG&A-Focused Optimization Actions

In response to current market conditions, the company is implementing actions that, once fully in place, will generate annual benefits of approximately $100 million.

Press Release article 3 of 5, Source: Company Website, 260 words

January 07: RPM Announces Acquisition of Kalzip

Acquisition expands RPM's global building envelope portfolio

MEDINA, Ohio--(BUSINESS WIRE)-- RPM International Inc. (NYSE:RPM) today announced that a definitive agreement has been signed for its Tremco Construction Products Group ("Tremco CPG") to acquire Kalzip GmbH ("Kalzip"), a global leader in the design and production of metal-based roofs and facades for building envelopes. The transaction will bolster Tremco CPG's building envelope systems capabilities with a premier brand known for architectural innovation and high-performance solutions that meet demanding specifications.

Kalzip is a globally recognized leader in aluminum roofing and facade systems. Headquartered in Koblenz, Germany, the company specializes in customized lightweight, weather-resistant building envelope technologies. Delivering over 1.2 billion square feet of aluminum solutions worldwide, key projects have included El Prat Airport roof refurbishment in Barcelona, Mercedes Benz Arena in Berlin, Children's Hospital Dublin and Ikea Dubai. Kalzip generated calendar year 2024 net sales of approximately EUR75 million. The acquisition enhances Tremco CPG's position in key international markets, including Europe and India, while creating future opportunities to expand Kalzip's aluminum systems to North American customers.

The addition of Kalzip represents a strategic enhancement to Tremco CPG's integrated portfolio of building envelope solutions, which includes roofing restoration and maintenance systems, field-applied and panelized EIFS, panel systems, sealants, waterproofing and more. This combination aligns with Tremco CPG's strategy to offer a seamless, end-to-end approach to construction that enhances building efficiency, durability and aesthetics.

The transaction is expected to close in the fourth quarter of fiscal 2026, subject to customary closing conditions.

January 06: RPM International quarterly dividends steady

RPM International today announced a quarterly dividend of 54.0c per share for Q2/2026; unchanged from the previous quarter (Q1/2026) dividend of 54.0c. The ex-dividend date is Friday, January 16, 2026 and the record date is Friday, January 16, 2026 and it is payable on Friday, January 30. Total dividends per share paid in the 12 months ended January 06 were $2.07.

December 05 2025: RPM to Announce Fiscal 2026 Second-Quarter Results on January 8, 2026

MEDINA, Ohio--(BUSINESS WIRE)-- RPM International Inc. (NYSE: RPM) announced today that it will release its financial results for the fiscal 2026 second quarter before the stock market opens on Thursday, January 8, 2026. The results will be issued via newswire and will also be available on the RPM website at www.RPMinc.com.

Management will host a conference call to discuss the results beginning at 10:00 a.m. Eastern Time the same day. The call can be accessed via webcast at www.RPMinc.com/Investors/Presentations-Webcasts/ or by dialing 844-481-2915, or 412-317-0708 for international callers. Participants are asked to call the assigned number approximately 10 minutes before the conference call begins and request to join the RPM International call. The call, which will last approximately one hour, will be open to the public, but only financial analysts will be permitted to ask questions. The media and all other participants will be in a listen-only mode.

For those unable to listen to the live call, a replay will be available from January 8, 2026, until January 15, 2026. The replay can be accessed by dialing 855-669-9658, or 412-317-0088 for international callers. The access code is 1320592. The call also will be available both live and for replay, and as a written transcript, via the RPM website at www.RPMinc.com.
